title: Live-Coding Playbook
headings: Live-Coding Playbook
The one job: make your thinking audible
1. The UMPIRE narration protocol
Beat 0 — Frame it (10 seconds, before anything)
Beat 1 — UNDERSTAND: clarify inputs, constraints, edge cases (2–4 min)
Beat 2 — MATCH: name the pattern (30 seconds)
Beat 3 — PLAN: state the approach AND its complexity before typing (2–3 min)
Beat 4 — IMPLEMENT: code in small, verbalized steps (bulk of the time)
Beat 5 — REVIEW: hand-trace an example + edge cases, out loud (~8–10 min, do NOT skip)
Beat 6 — EVALUATE: 3-sentence complexity wrap-up + one improvement (2–3 min)
2. Recovery & hints playbook
When you're stuck — never go silent
When given a hint — treat it as gold
Think out loud without rambling
When you spot your own bug
3. Ranked DS&A pattern table
Tier 1 — expect one of these; drill hardest
Tier 2 — very plausible
Tier 3 — possible; know the shape, don't over-invest
4. Debug this DSA code
Bug 1 — Off-by-one loop bound
Bug 2 — Missing seen[0] = 1 in a prefix-sum count
Bug 3 — Storing the value, not the index, in Two Sum
Bug 4 — Mutating a list while iterating it
Bug 5 — pop(0) as a BFS queue
5. One ML-flavored warm-up (narrate the intent)
Python fluency signals (reach for these)
The one-line summary to internalize
excerpt: Your specific risk profile: you know the algorithms and you're AI-native, so the two things that are actually rusty are (a) raw unaided typing/recall with no autocomplete, and (b) narrating out loud while your hands move. This playbook dril…

title: ML From Scratch (Python)
headings: ML From Scratch (Python)
1. Softmax (numerically stable) + Cross-Entropy — #1 most likely
2. Scaled Dot-Product Attention — #2, they're a Foundation-Models team
3. Precision / Recall / F1 + rank-based AUC
4. BPE merge step — directly relevant: eBay ships a custom e-commerce tokenizer
5. Welford running mean/variance + z-score normalize — what LayerNorm does internally
6. k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN)
7. k-Means (guard the empty cluster)
8. Logistic Regression + Gradient Descent
DEBUG THIS BROKEN MODEL
Bug 1 — Missing max-subtraction in softmax
Bug 2 — Wrong reduction axis
Bug 3 — Missing keepdims
Bug 4 — Off-by-one causal mask (the dangerous one)
Bug 5 — Gradient sign error
30-second pre-flight checklist (run this mentally before you say "done")
excerpt: An ML-research round often swaps the LeetCode puzzle for "implement this in NumPy and overfit it on toy data" or "here's a broken model — find the bug." Fluency here should match your regular-Python fluency; fumbling the softmax formula for…

title: Engineering Q&A — Build & Train
headings: Engineering Q&A — Build & Train
TOPIC 1 — Docker & Containers for ML
Q1.1 — Why containerize an ML application at all?
Q1.2 — How do you get GPU access inside a container?
Q1.3 — Your ML image is 15 GB. How do you shrink it?
Q1.4 — Walk me through a good Dockerfile layer strategy.

Q1.6 — How do you handle secrets and config in containers?
Q1.7 — Container vs VM, briefly?
TOPIC 2 — Testing ML Systems
Q2.1 — How is testing an ML system different from testing normal software?
Q2.2 — What would you actually unit-test in an ML pipeline?
Q2.3 — How do you handle non-determinism so tests don't flake?
Q2.4 — Unit vs integration tests here — what does each look like?
Q2.5 — How do you validate the data, not just the code?
Q2.6 — Beyond accuracy, how do you test model behavior?
Q2.7 — How do you keep tests fast enough for CI?
TOPIC 3 — CI/CD for ML
Q3.1 — What does CI/CD look like for ML, and how's it different?
Q3.2 — Data and models are too big for Git. How do you version them?
Q3.3 — How do you make an ML result reproducible?
Q3.4 — What runs in a GitHub Actions workflow for an ML repo?
Q3.5 — Would you actually train a model inside CI?
Q3.6 — How do you deploy a new model version safely?
Q3.7 — Where do MLflow and DVC actually fit together?
TOPIC 4 — Distributed & Efficient Training (Infra Optimization)
Q4.1 — Explain the memory footprint of training a large model.
Q4.2 — The fifth bucket is activations — why do they matter and how do you control them?
Q4.3 — DDP vs FSDP — what's the difference and when do you use each?
Q4.4 — Explain DeepSpeed ZeRO stages 1, 2, and 3.
Q4.5 — Tensor, pipeline, data, and sequence parallelism — and when do you combine them?
Q4.6 — bf16 vs fp16 for mixed precision — does it matter?
Q4.7 — What is MFU and why do we care?
Q4.8 — Warmup and cosine decay — why, and what changes for continued pretraining?
Q4.9 — Gradient accumulation — what is it and when do you reach for it?
Q4.10 — What is FlashAttention and why did it matter?
Q4.11 — How do you checkpoint and resume a long training run robustly?
The production-ops gap

title: Engineering Q&A — Serve & Monitor
headings: Engineering Q&A — Serve & Monitor
How to use this doc
Section A — Inference Optimization
A1. Walk me through how you'd cut the inference cost of a 7B LLM in production.
A2. What is the KV cache and why does it dominate inference memory?
A3. Explain PagedAttention and why it matters.
A4. Continuous (in-flight) batching vs. static batching?
A5. When does batching stop helping?
A6. FP8 vs INT8 vs INT4 — how do you choose?
A7. AWQ vs GPTQ vs GGUF?
A8. What is speculative decoding and what's the catch?
A9. GQA and MQA — what problem do they solve?
A10. Prefill vs decode — why talk about them separately?
A11. What does torch.compile buy you, and where does ONNX fit?
A12. When would you distill instead of quantize?
Section B — Serving & Deployment
B1. Which serving stack would you pick and why?
B2. REST vs gRPC for model serving?
B3. Walk me through a safe rollout of a new model version.
B4. Canary vs A/B testing vs shadow — aren't they the same?
B5. How do you roll back a bad deploy?
B6. What is a model registry and why do you need one?
B7. How do you autoscale GPU inference?
B8. What's a feature store and when does it matter?
B9. How would you serve eBay's LiLiuM or e-Llama efficiently?
B10. One big model or a fleet of small ones?
Section C — Monitoring & Observability
C1. What would you put on a serving dashboard for an LLM endpoint?
C2. Data drift vs concept drift — and how do you detect each?
C3. How does PSI work and what thresholds do you use?
C4. The KS test — when and why?
C5. Labels arrive late — how do you monitor quality in the meantime?
C6. How do you monitor a generative model with no clean label?
C7. What do logging, metrics, and tracing each give you?
C8. How would you set up alerting without drowning in noise?
C9. What's a feedback loop and how can it go wrong?
C10. First 24 hours after a model goes live — what do you watch?
Cheat Block — reason from these live
1. KV-cache memory (the one formula to know cold)
2. Batching throughput intuition (one sentence)
3. Quantization tradeoff table (fast recall)
4. Cost / latency framing (talk in unit economics)
Tying in your work — routing, MERA, TwinRouterBench, pruning
excerpt: Three rules baked into every answer: 1. Lead with conceptual command. Say the formula, the number, or the mechanic first — that proves domain fluency before you explain. 2. Own the ops gap in one clause, then pivot. Live canary, real-time d…

title: STAR Stories — The Four Prompts
headings: STAR Stories — The Four Prompts
How to use this doc
P1 — A challenging project you delivered
PRIMARY · VR eye-tracking emotion dataset (the hardware wall)
BACKUP · Pruning nnU-Net into MIDL on a hard deadline
P2 — A difficult interaction with a customer / vendor / stakeholder
OPTION A · Academic collaboration — a disagreement on scope / direction / authorship
OPTION B · Industry collaboration — a partner's priorities vs. what the research needed
P3 — A time you used your technical expertise to advise
PRIMARY · TwinRouterBench — "measure it, don't argue it"
BACKUP · The ML voice on a physics-led MRI team (Reverse Imaging)
P4 — A time you used data to influence a decision
PRIMARY · The ACDC ground-truth curve behind "80% removed"
BACKUP · The accounting choice that flipped a router ranking (TwinRouterBench)
Leadership style (~150 words spoken)
Genuine failure — MERA's null result, and the metric that could have lied
Consistency note — don't tell one project two contradictory ways
